title: Minimizing Access Paths
breadcrumbs: Design Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Minimizing Access Paths The system load caused by the access paths is expressed as access stress rates. You can shorten access paths and minimize access stress rates by storing redundant data to increase ease of access (denormalization) or changing the user view. Denormalization has a major impact on the application model and therefore on the application. Denormalization is usually considered before changing the user-view, because the latter affects the information processing capabilities offered to the end-users. Denormalization measures include the following: Adding redundant relationships between entities of the Uniface application model Combining separate entities into a single entity Splitting an entity horizontally by dividing different sets of rows in separate entities Splitting an entity vertically by dividing different sets of columns in separate entities Replicating entities Temporarily isolating rows in a separate entity—isolating a set of related rows of different entities into a single entity for intensive processing Adding redundant attributes, for example, for storing processing results or storing state information Although it is an effective way to improve performance, denormalization should be applied carefully because it makes the application harder to maintain. Also, a denormalized application model no longer reflects the business correctly. The listed denormalization measures are modifications to the data model. Several products implement denormalization on a more technical level, leaving the data model as it is. For example, some databases support table replication, and data warehousing products temporarily isolate data. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
